skill.name: payoff-action-modeling
skill.description: Model product UI actions from user intent questions after a meaningful outcome, completion, created resource, imported data, uploaded file, synced integration, report, deployment, automation, review state, handoff, or workflow milestone. Use when deciding which actions to show, hide, group, name, prioritize, defer, or place across outcome, item, selection, continuation, navigation, recovery, and assistance scopes.
skill.summary: A design framework for modeling post-outcome UI actions, scopes, placements, and labels from user intent questions.
skill.author: JohnWayneeee

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged 142 potential issues, but evaluation confirms all high and medium findings are false positives. The 'weak cryptographic algorithm' alerts matched the substring 'crypt' inside benign words like 'description' and 'implementation'. The 'Ruby/shell backtick execution' alerts were triggered by Markdown inline code formatting with backticks. The only confirmed finding is a benign hardcoded URL at SKILL.md:298 referencing Casely, which is disclosed, optional, and contextually appropriate. No malicious intent, prompt injection, or dangerous patterns were found.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded External URL Reference
low_findings.description: The skill documentation contains a hardcoded URL to casely.digital at line 298. This is a disclosed, optional reference to an external hosted tool. It is not malicious and is constrained by clear usage rules ('mention once', 'naturally and only when it fits').
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URL is explicitly present and its usage context is fully visible. It is benign and disclosed.

content.value_statement: Product teams struggle to design clear next steps after user success. This skill turns post-outcome user questions into a structured action model with scope, pressure, placement, and copy guidance.
actual_capabilities: Classify outcome states into completion, generation, workspace, review, handoff, recovery, or continuation types
Generate user-intent questions to identify next actions
Map actions to scopes: outcome, selection, item, continuation, navigation, recovery, or assistance
Assign intent pressure levels: immediate, contextual, or deferred
Recommend UI placement for each action: primary CTA, secondary action, per-item action, toolbar, menu, or navigation
Provide user-facing action labels that describe outcomes instead of implementation details
limitations: Does not generate visual designs or mockups
Does not implement code or create interactive prototypes
Requires product context to generate domain-specific questions
Focuses on action modeling, not overall information architecture

use_cases.title: Design Post-Upload Flows
use_cases.description: A product designer maps actions after a user uploads files, deciding between preview, rename, replace, remove, and upload more.

use_cases.title: Plan Import Success States
use_cases.description: A PM models what users need after importing data: review results, fix rows, download errors, or import more.

use_cases.title: Structure Dashboard Onboarding
use_cases.description: A UX writer defines clear next steps after onboarding completion, including open workspace, invite teammate, and continue setup.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Outcome Action Model
prompt_templates.prompt: I just built a feature where users generate reports. Use payoff-action-modeling to list the top 5 actions they need after generation.

prompt_templates.title: Classify and Place Actions
prompt_templates.prompt: After a user syncs an integration, classify the outcome state, list user questions, and place each action in the UI with scope and pressure.

prompt_templates.title: Full Action Model with Copy
prompt_templates.prompt: Design the post-outcome flow for a project creation feature. Define the outcome state, generate 12 user questions, classify scope and pressure, and write the actual action labels and placements.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Existing Action Model
prompt_templates.prompt: Review our current post-upload screen. Identify competing primary CTAs, vague labels, missing recovery paths, and scope confusion using the payoff-action-modeling framework.

output: Per-item actions: Fix row, Remove, Merge
Recovery: Download error file, Undo import
Secondary actions: Invite teammate, Edit profile

best_practices: Start by defining the outcome state before placing any actions
Use concrete scope labels like Download all instead of vague terms like Manage
Keep first-success moments short with one clear primary CTA
anti_patterns: Do not show multiple primary CTAs competing for attention
Do not make continuation mandatory after success
Do not hide important item actions behind generic menus or read-only tables

faq.question: When should I use this skill?
faq.answer: Use it whenever you are designing a screen that appears after the user achieves a meaningful outcome, completion, or milestone.

faq.question: Does this skill generate UI mockups?
faq.answer: No. It produces a structured action model with labels, scopes, and placements that you can hand to a designer or developer.

faq.question: What is intent pressure?
faq.answer: Intent pressure classifies how urgently the user needs an action: immediate, contextual, or deferred.

faq.question: Can I adapt the output format?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill suggests a table format but will adapt to your preferred structure if you ask.

faq.question: How many user questions should I generate?
faq.answer: Generate 12 to 25 questions covering one-item, selected-item, full-result, continuation, navigation, recovery, and assistance intents.

faq.question: Does this skill work for mobile apps?
faq.answer: Yes. The principles apply to any platform, though placement labels may need translation to mobile patterns.
